name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِذَا نُودِىَ لِلصَّلَوٰةِ مِن يَوْمِ ٱلْجُمُعَةِ فَٱسْعَوْا۟ إِلَىٰ ذِكْرِ ٱللَّهِ وَذَرُوا۟ ٱلْبَيْعَ ۚ ذَٰلِكُمْ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ إِن كُنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ (٩)
```

```english
O you who have faith! When the call is made for prayer on Friday, hurry toward the remembrance of Allah, and leave all business. That is better for you, should you know. (9)
```

```arabic
فَإِذَا قُضِيَتِ ٱلصَّلَوٰةُ فَٱنتَشِرُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَٱبْتَغُوا۟ مِن فَضْلِ ٱللَّهِ وَٱذْكُرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ كَثِيرًا لَّعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ (١٠)
```

```english
And when the prayer is finished disperse through the land and seek Allah’s grace, and remember Allah much so that you may be felicitous. (10)
```

```arabic
وَإِذَا رَأَوْا۟ تِجَـٰرَةً أَوْ لَهْوًا ٱنفَضُّوٓا۟ إِلَيْهَا وَتَرَكُوكَ قَآئِمًا ۚ قُلْ مَا عِندَ ٱللَّهِ خَيْرٌ مِّنَ ٱللَّهْوِ وَمِنَ ٱلتِّجَـٰرَةِ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ خَيْرُ ٱلرَّٰزِقِينَ (١١)
```

```english
When they sight a deal or a diversion, they scatter off towards it and leave you standing! Say, ‘What is with Allah is better than diversion and dealing, and Allah is the best of providers.’ (11)
```

## (Commentary)

This section emphasizes the obligation of the Friday prayer (صلاة الجمعة) and the prohibition of trade (البيع) during its performance. It contains a rebuke for those who dispersed toward diversion and trade at that time, expressing disapproval of their conduct.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِذا نُودِيَ لِلصَّلاةِ مِنْ يَوْمِ الْجُمُعَةِ فَاسْعَوْا إِلى ذِكْرِ اللهِ وَذَرُوا الْبَيْعَ** **"O you who have faith! When the call is made for prayer on Friday, hurry toward the remembrance of Allah, and leave all business"** (62:9) etc. What is meant by the "call for prayer on Friday" is the *adhan* (الأذان), as in His saying: **وَإِذا نادَيْتُمْ إِلَى الصَّلاةِ اتَّخَذُوها هُزُواً وَلَعِباً** "When you make the call for prayer, they take it in derision and play" (5:58).

The word *al-jumu‘ah* (الجمعة) — with a *dammah* on both the *jim* and *mim*, or a *dammah* followed by a *sukun* — refers to one of the days of the week. It was originally called *Yawm al-‘Arubah*, but the name *al-Jumu‘ah* eventually became dominant. What is meant by "prayer on Friday" is the Friday prayer (صلاة الجمعة) prescribed for that day. "Hurrying" (السعي) refers to walking with speed. "The remembrance of Allah" (ذكر الله) refers to the prayer, as in His saying: **وَلَذِكْرُ اللهِ أَكْبَرُ** "and the remembrance of Allah is surely greater" (29:45), according to what has been said. Others have said it refers to the sermon (الخطبة) before the prayer. His saying: **وَذَرُوا الْبَيْعَ** "and leave all business" is a command to abandon it. Based on what the context suggests, it means a prohibition against engaging in any activity that distracts from the Friday prayer, whether it be trade or otherwise. The prohibition was specifically linked to trade (البيع) because it is among the most prominent instances of what distracts one from prayer.

The meaning is: O you who have faith, when the *adhan* is called for the Friday prayer on its day, be diligent in walking to the prayer and abandon trade and everything that distracts you from it.

His saying: **ذلِكُمْ خَيْرٌ لَكُمْ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ** **"That is better for you, should you know"** (62:9) is an exhortation and an inducement for them regarding what He commanded of prayer and the abandonment of trade.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَإِذا قُضِيَتِ الصَّلاةُ فَانْتَشِرُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ وَابْتَغُوا مِنْ فَضْلِ اللهِ** **"Then, when the prayer is finished, disperse through the land and seek Allah’s grace"** (62:10) etc. What is meant by the "prayer being finished" is the performance of the Friday prayer. "Dispersing through the land" (الانتشار في الأرض) means scattering therein. "Seeking Allah’s grace" (ابتغاء فضل الله) refers to seeking sustenance (الرزق), considering its contrast with the abandonment of trade in the previous verse. However, it was previously mentioned that the intent is to abandon everything that distracts from the Friday prayer. Accordingly, "seeking Allah’s grace" refers to seeking His gift in a general sense through dispersing to seek His sustenance via buying and selling, as well as seeking His reward by visiting the sick, striving to fulfill the need of a fellow Muslim, visiting a brother in Allah, attending a gathering of knowledge, and the like.

His saying: **فَانْتَشِرُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ** "disperse through the land" is a command occurring after a prohibition, thus it signifies permissibility (الجواز) and lawfulness (الإباحة) rather than obligation (الوجوب). The same applies to His saying: **وَابْتَغُوا** "and seek" and **وَاذْكُرُوا** "and remember."

His saying: **وَاذْكُرُوا اللهَ كَثِيراً لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ** **"and remember Allah greatly so that you may come to felicity"** (62:10). What is meant by "remembrance" (الذكر) is broader than verbal remembrance; it includes remembering Him, the Exalted, in the heart by turning toward Him inwardly. Felicity (الفلاح) — *the attainment of salvation from all misery* — in this context, considering the preceding discussion on purification (التزكية) and teaching (التعليم) and the severe rebuke in the following verse, refers to spiritual growth and knowledge. This is because frequent remembrance causes the meaning of the One remembered to become firmly rooted in the soul and inscribed in the mind, thereby severing the roots of heedlessness (الغفلة) and bequeathing religious godwariness (التقوى), which is the likely ground for felicity. The Exalted said: **وَاتَّقُوا اللهَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ** "and be wary of Allah so that you may come to felicity" (3:200).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَإِذا رَأَوْا تِجارَةً أَوْ لَهْواً انْفَضُّوا إِلَيْها وَتَرَكُوكَ قائِماً** **"When they sight some merchandise or diversion, they scatter toward it and leave you standing"** (62:11) etc. "Scattering" (الانفضاض) — according to what al-Raghib mentioned — is a metaphor for breaking apart and the separation of parts from one another.

The narrations of both the Shi‘a and the Sunnis agree that a caravan (عير) carrying merchandise arrived in Madinah on a Friday while the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله was standing and delivering the sermon. They beat the drums and tambourines to inform the people, so the people in the mosque scattered toward them and left the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله standing and delivering the sermon; thus, the verse was revealed. So, "diversion" (اللهو) refers to the use of musical instruments and tools of entertainment to gather people for trade. The pronoun in "toward it" (إِلَيْها) refers to the merchandise (التجارة) because it was the primary objective, while the diversion was intended for its sake. It has also been said that the pronoun refers to one of the two, as if it were said: "they scattered toward it [the diversion] and they scattered toward it [the merchandise]." This is because each of them was a reason for the people to scatter toward it and gather around it. For this reason, He used the disjunctive "or" between them, saying: **تِجارَةً أَوْ لَهْواً** "merchandise or diversion," and did not say "merchandise and diversion." The pronoun is suitable to refer to either because "diversion" (اللهو) is originally a verbal noun (مصدر) for which both masculine and feminine genders are permissible.

For this reason also, He considered "what is with Allah" (ما عِنْدَ اللهِ) better than each of them individually, saying: **مِنَ اللهْوِ وَمِنَ التِّجارَةِ** "than diversion and than merchandise," and did not say "than diversion and merchandise."

His saying: **قُلْ ما عِنْدَ اللهِ خَيْرٌ مِنَ اللهْوِ وَمِنَ التِّجارَةِ وَاللهُ خَيْرُ الرَّازِقِينَ** **"Say, ‘What is with Allah is better than diversion and merchandise, and Allah is the best of providers’"** (62:11) is a command to the Prophet to alert them to their error in what they did — and how heinous it was. What is meant by "what is with Allah" is the reward (الثواب) that follows listening to the sermon and the exhortation.

The meaning is: Say to them: What is with Allah of reward is better than diversion and merchandise, because His reward is a true, eternal, and uninterrupted good, whereas the good found in diversion and merchandise is an imaginary, vanishing, and false matter, and it may even entail His wrath, as is the case with diversion.

It has been said that "better" (خير) is used in the verse stripped of the meaning of comparison (التفضيل), as in His saying, the Exalted: **أَأَرْبابٌ مُتَفَرِّقُونَ خَيْرٌ أَمِ اللهُ الْواحِدُ الْقَهَّارُ** "Are discrete lords better, or Allah, the One, the All-paramount?" (12:39), and this is common in usage.

In the verse — meaning His saying: **وَإِذا رَأَوْا** "When they sight" — there is a transition (التفات) from direct address to the third person (الغيبة). The subtle point (النكتة) in this is to emphasize the rebuke and the disapproval of the act suggested by the context, by turning away from honoring them with direct address and leaving them in the state of absence (الغيبة), where their Lord does not face them with His Noble Countenance.

This turning away is also hinted at in His saying: **قُلْ ما عِنْدَ اللهِ خَيْرٌ** "Say, ‘What is with Allah is better’," where He did not specify to whom he should say it; He did not say "Say to them," just as He mentioned them with their pronoun at first without a preceding referent, saying: **وَإِذا رَأَوْا** "When they sight," relying on the indication of the context.

"The Best of Providers" (خير الرازقين) is among the Beautiful Names of Allah, like "The Provider" (الرزاق). The discussion on the meaning of "sustenance" (الرزق) has already preceded.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Faqih*, it is narrated: "In Madinah, when the *mu’adhin* called the *adhan* on Friday, a caller would announce: 'Trade is forbidden,' because of the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِذا نُودِيَ لِلصَّلاةِ مِنْ يَوْمِ الْجُمُعَةِ فَاسْعَوْا إِلى ذِكْرِ اللهِ وَذَرُوا الْبَيْعَ** (O you who have faith! When the call is made for prayer on Friday, hurry toward the remembrance of Allah, and leave all business)."

**I say:** It was also narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from Ibn Abi Shaybah, ‘Abd ibn Humayd, and Ibn al-Mundhir, from Maymun ibn Mahran, and its wording is: "In Madinah, when the *mu’adhin* called the *adhan* on Friday, they would call out in the markets: 'Trade is forbidden! Trade is forbidden!'"

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi: Regarding His saying: **فَاسْعَوْا إِلى ذِكْرِ اللهِ** (hurry toward the remembrance of Allah), he said: "It means speed in walking." In a narration by Abu al-Jarud from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him) regarding the verse, it is said: "**فاسعوا** (hurry) means 'go forth'; and it is said: '**اسعوا** (strive) means 'act for it,' which is trimming the mustache, plucking the armpits, clipping the nails, performing the ritual bath (الغسل), wearing the cleanest clothes, and applying perfume for Friday. This is the 'striving' (السعي) of which Allah says: **وَمَنْ أَرادَ الْآخِرَةَ وَسَعى لَها سَعْيَها وَهُوَ مُؤْمِنٌ** (Whoever desires the Hereafter and strives for it with an effort worthy of it, should he be faithful)."

**I say:** He means that "striving" (السعي) is not merely speed in walking.

In *al-Majma‘*, Anas narrated from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله that he said regarding His saying: **فَإِذا قُضِيَتِ الصَّلاةُ فَانْتَشِرُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ** (Then, when the prayer is finished, disperse through the land) — "It is not for seeking the world, but for visiting the sick, attending a funeral, and visiting a brother in Allah."

**I say:** It was also narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from Ibn Jarir from Anas from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, and from Ibn Mardawayh from Ibn ‘Abbas from him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله.

In the same source, it is narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) that he said: "The prayer is on Friday, and the dispersing is on Saturday."

**I say:** There are other narrations with this meaning.

In the same source, ‘Umar ibn Yazid narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) who said: "I indeed ride out for a need that Allah has already sufficed, and I only ride for it seeking that Allah may see me striving in the morning in search of the lawful (الحلال). Have you not heard the saying of Allah, exalted is His Name: **فَإِذا قُضِيَتِ الصَّلاةُ فَانْتَشِرُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ وَابْتَغُوا مِنْ فَضْلِ اللهِ** (Then, when the prayer is finished, disperse through the land and seek Allah’s grace)?"

"Do you think that if a man entered a house and plastered its door shut, then said: 'My sustenance will descend upon me,' that this would happen? Indeed, he is one of the three whose supplication is not answered."

He said: I asked: "Who are these?" He replied: "A man who has a wife and prays against her; his prayer is not answered because her bond is in his hand — if he wished, he could set her free. And a man who has a right over another man but does not take witnesses against him, so the other denies him his right; he prays against him but is not answered because he abandoned what he was commanded to do. And a man who has something [wealth] but sits in his house and does not disperse, nor seek, nor search until he consumes it, then he supplicates and is not answered."

In the same source, Jabir ibn ‘Abdullah said: "A caravan arrived while we were praying with the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, so the people scattered toward it until only twelve men remained, of whom I was one. Then the verse was revealed: **وَإِذا رَأَوْا تِجارَةً أَوْ لَهْواً** (When they sight some merchandise or diversion)."

From *‘Awali al-La’ali*, Muqatil ibn Sulayman narrated: "While the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله was delivering the sermon on Friday, Dihyah al-Kalbi arrived from Syria with merchandise. Whenever he arrived, no young woman (عاتق) remained in Madinah but that she came to him. He used to bring, whenever he arrived, everything the people needed — of flour, wheat, and other things. Then he would beat the drum to inform the people of his arrival, and the people would go out and buy from him."

"He arrived one Friday — and this was before he had embraced Islam — while the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله was delivering the sermon on the pulpit. The people went out, and none remained in the mosque except twelve. The Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: 'Were it not for these, stones would have been marked against them from the sky.' And Allah revealed the verse in Surah al-Jumu‘ah."

**I say:** The story is narrated through many chains from both the Shi‘a and the Sunnis, and the reports differ regarding the number of those who remained in the mosque, ranging from seven to forty.

In the same source: "**انْفَضُّوا** (they scattered)" means they dispersed. It is narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) that he said: "They departed toward it and left you standing, delivering the sermon on the pulpit."

Jabir ibn Samurah said: "I never saw the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله deliver a sermon except while he was standing. So, whoever tells you that he delivered a sermon while sitting, deem him a liar."

**I say:** This is also narrated in other reports.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Abi Shaybah recorded from Tawus who said: "The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله delivered the sermon standing, as did Abu Bakr, ‘Umar, and ‘Uthman. The first one to sit on the pulpit was Mu‘awiyah ibn Abi Sufyan."
